stress_regime_preliminary.regime_change_evidence: Tariff Shock Cascade Protocol activated by April 2 Liberation Day package (effective US tariff rate now exceeds 15% with disputes active across EU, China, Canada, Mexico, Japan, Korea simultaneously). Energy shock inflation expectations repriced upward (5-year breakeven +26bps since Middle East conflict onset, highest since Feb 2025). Fed and ECB both signalling caution: Fed divided on cuts (12/19 expect cuts vs 7 expect none; futures price 60% probability no cuts through 2026); ECB holds citing material near-term inflation risks from energy. These developments span three indicator domains simultaneously: trade/tariff disruption (activated), inflation/central bank posture (repriced), and sovereign debt/fiscal sustainability (rate path uncertainty).

indicator_domains.signal: US 5-year breakeven inflation rises 26bps to highest level since February 2025; ECB and Fed both cite energy-driven upside inflation risks
indicator_domains.key_data_point: US breakeven inflation +26bps since Middle East conflict onset; ECB cites material near-term inflation pass-through; Fed dot plot split 12-7 on cuts
indicator_domains.source: Deloitte Global Economic Outlook Weekly Update, FOMC dot plot, ECB statement

indicator_domains.signal: Trump April 2 Liberation Day tariff package activates Tariff Shock Cascade Protocol; effective US tariff rate reaches highest level since 1930s

indicator_domains.source: USTR official statement; Peterson Institute PIIE tariff tracker; GMM Tariff Shock Cascade Protocol activation criterion met

indicator_domains.signal: Fed and ECB both cite downside growth risks from geopolitical energy shock; caution embedded in forward guidance
indicator_domains.key_data_point: ECB statement explicitly notes downside growth risks alongside upside inflation; tariff shock adds demand destruction channel for export-sensitive economies
indicator_domains.source: ECB policy statement; Fed Powell testimony

indicator_domains.signal: Energy shock and tariff uncertainty create elevated FX volatility backdrop; EME currencies face capital outflow pressure from export tariff exposure
indicator_domains.key_data_point: Dollar weaponisation via tariff escalation; emerging markets with export exposure (Vietnam, Mexico, Bangladesh) structurally vulnerable
indicator_domains.source: GMM Tariff Shock Cascade Protocol sector exposure mapping; daily collector cross-monitor relevance flags

indicator_domains.signal: Global rate cycle divergence widens; US higher-for-longer bias conflicts with EU easing pressure; emerging market refinancing stress potential
indicator_domains.key_data_point: Futures market prices 60% probability of no Fed cuts through 2026; ECB rate expectations revised higher; EM sovereign spreads face pressure from tariff disruption
indicator_domains.source: CME FedWatch; Deloitte Insights; IIF debt monitoring framework

indicator_domains.signal: Tariff shock and energy price volatility create corporate earnings repricing risks; fixed-income and equity volatility likely to elevate
indicator_domains.key_data_point: Export-sensitive sector earnings revisions under tariff shock now flagged as standing check under active protocol; energy input cost inflation embedded
indicator_domains.source: GMM Tariff Shock Cascade Protocol standing checks; FactSet earnings revision tracking

central_bank_tracker.last_action: Held rates at meeting ending April 2026; voted 11-1 to hold
central_bank_tracker.forward_guidance: Powell emphasizes uncertainty from energy prices and geopolitical risk; dot plot shows 12 of 19 members expect at least one cut in 2026, but 7 expect no cuts; CME implied probability of zero cuts through end-2026 at 60%

central_bank_tracker.last_action: Held benchmark rates unchanged at April 2026 meeting
central_bank_tracker.forward_guidance: Middle East conflict creates upside inflation risks via energy and downside growth risks via demand destruction; investor rate expectations revised higher; ECB maintaining caution on easing cycle initiation
Face downside growth pressures from US tariff escalation (export-oriented economy); inflation backdrop supportive of neutral bias but tariff shock creates asset price volatility
Energy shock inflation expectations repriced higher; investor rate expectations revised upward alongside ECB; awaiting corporate earnings and labour market data before signalling easing
Tariff shock targets Japanese exporters directly (Section 232 steel/aluminium tariffs, broader IP tariffs); policy divergence with Fed and ECB widens

tariff_tracker.target: Global (EU, China, Canada, Mexico, Japan, South Korea prioritised)
tariff_tracker.measure: April 2 Liberation Day comprehensive tariff package; effective US tariff rate raised to highest level since 1930s (exceeds 15% weighted average); covers steel/aluminium (Section 232), technology hardware, consumer goods, agricultural products
tariff_tracker.macro_impact_assessment: Tariff Shock Cascade Protocol activated. Direct: input cost inflation in steel, aluminium, semiconductors. Indirect: export-volume compression in Asia (Vietnam, Mexico, Bangladesh, Korea, Japan), demand destruction for tariff-exposed EME economies. Global supply chain dislocation and inventory frontloading effects. Retaliation chains expected within 30-60 days. Amplifies energy shock inflation backdrop via cost-pass mechanisms. Stagflation risk elevated for G10 ex-US (growth headwind + tariff inflation premium).

tail_risks.risk: Tariff-inflation feedback loop: tariff-driven input cost pass-through triggers wage-price spiral, forcing longer-duration of Fed higher-for-longer bias, collapsing equity valuations and credit spreads globally
tail_risks.trigger_condition: PCE/core PCE re-accelerates above 3.0% YoY within 8 weeks; corporate earnings guidance downside revisions exceed 20% breadth in export sectors; Fed dot plot tilts hawkish in June meeting

tail_risks.risk: Energy shock persistence: Middle East conflict duration extends beyond 90 days, oil prices remain elevated (>$100/bbl), triggering cascading central bank policy errors (ECB rate hikes into recession, Fed forced higher) and emerging market currency crises
tail_risks.trigger_condition: Brent crude sustained above $95/bbl; shipping disruption (Hormuz, Red Sea) extends; EM central banks face reserve outflows requiring emergency rate hikes; currency basis swaps dislocate

tail_risks.risk: Tariff retaliation cascade: EU and China enact symmetric retaliatory tariffs on US exports (agriculture, semiconductors, industrial goods), triggering demand shock to US corporate earnings and municipal tax bases in agricultural/export regions
tail_risks.trigger_condition: WTO dispute filings from EU and China within 14 days of April 2 package; first retaliatory measures announced within 30 days; sector-specific lobby pressure forces US political concessions or escalation

tail_risks.risk: Treasury market dislocation: Tariff shock + higher-for-longer Fed bias triggers Treasury basis trade unwinding, SOFR-IORB spread blowout, and collateral scarcity for systemically important financial institutions
tail_risks.trigger_condition: On-the-run Treasury bid-offer spreads widen >2bps sustained; basis trade overhang signals from primary dealers; SOFR-IORB spread breaches +50bps; repo haircuts increase

tail_risks.risk: EM currency crisis contagion: Tariff disruption of export revenues + energy shock capex diversion triggers EM central bank policy tightening, capital outflows, and currency weakness in vulnerable economies (Fragile Five: India, Brazil, Indonesia, Turkey, South Africa)
tail_risks.trigger_condition: EMBI GD spread >500bps; Fragile Five aggregate currency weakness >10% YoY; EM central bank emergency meetings; IMF bailout discussions initiated

key_judgments.judgment: Tariff Shock Cascade Protocol activation materially elevates stagflation risk for G10 economies ex-US through end-Q2 2026. Energy shock provides inflation tail, tariffs provide growth headwind, and central bank policy error risk widens.

Fed and ECB policy divergence has widened materially: Fed bias toward higher-for-longer (12/19 dot plot cut expectation; 60% futures prob no cuts 2026), ECB caution amid energy shock inflation. This divergence supports dollar appreciation and European bond selloff through Q2.

Export-oriented emerging markets (Vietnam, Mexico, Bangladesh, South Korea, Taiwan) face simultaneous shocks: tariff demand destruction, energy cost inflation, and capital flight pressure. Tariff Shock Cascade Protocol mapping indicates EM equity stress and currency weakness are near-term (30d) probabilities.

Energy shock duration and tariff shock overlap create non-linear tail risk for corporate earnings revisions and sovereign refinancing stress. If both persist >90 days and interact via margin compression mechanics, systemic financial stability risk escalates from Amber to Red.

AI infrastructure capex as countervailing force is marginal in 2026 backdrop. Tariff shock applies to semiconductor supply chains; energy shock raises hyperscaler data centre electricity costs. AI capex upside is not a near-term offset to tariff/energy downside.

weekly_brief_draft:
Global macro stress regime has downgraded from Green to Amber across major economies and Red for emerging markets, driven by two simultaneous shocks materialising in the week of April 2-6, 2026. The Trump administration activated the Tariff Shock Cascade Protocol on April 2 via the Liberation Day comprehensive tariff package, raising the effective US tariff rate to its highest level since the 1930s and exceeding the 15 percent threshold for protocol activation. Concurrently, the Middle East conflict has repriced inflation expectations globally: US 5-year breakeven inflation has risen 26 basis points since conflict onset, hitting its highest level since February 2025. These two shocks interact dangerously across three macro indicator domains: tariff disruption, inflation dynamics, and central bank policy stance.

Inflation and Central Bank Posture domain shows marked deterioration. US 5-year breakeven inflation has repriced sharply upward, signalling energy shock pass-through. The Fed held rates at its April meeting with a 11-1 vote, but the dot plot reveals deep internal disagreement: 12 of 19 members expect at least one cut in 2026, while 7 expect no cuts. CME futures price only 60 percent probability of a rate cut by end-2026, pointing to a higher-for-longer regime. The ECB similarly held rates unchanged, explicitly citing Middle East conflict as creating upside inflation risks and downside growth risks simultaneously—a classic stagflation signal. The BoJ faces tariff headwinds directly (Section 232 steel/aluminium tariffs, broader IP tariffs on Japanese exporters). Central bank divergence has widened: the Fed is biased neutral-to-tight, the ECB is neutral-caution, and the BoJ faces export demand destruction.

Trade and Tariff Disruption domain has escalated to Red stress. The April 2 Liberation Day package activates the Tariff Shock Cascade Protocol because the US now has tariff disputes simultaneously active with six major trading partners (EU, China, Canada, Mexico, Japan, South Korea). Direct impacts include input cost inflation in steel, aluminium, and semiconductors. Indirect impacts include volume compression for export-oriented emerging markets—particularly Vietnam, Mexico, Bangladesh, South Korea, and Taiwan—where tariff exposure is acute. Retaliation chains are structurally probable within 30-60 days.

Emerging Markets face a compounding tail risk: export disruption from tariffs combines with energy cost shocks and currency pressure from capital outflows. The Fragile Five (India, Brazil, Indonesia, Turkey, South Africa) are most exposed. Sovereign debt stress is likely to rise as refinancing costs increase and fiscal revenues contract. The global financial stability domain shows early stress signals in volatility repricing and equity earnings revision breadth beginning to deteriorate in export sectors.

Key near-term risks: (1) tariff-inflation feedback loop triggering wage-price acceleration and forcing extended Fed tightness; (2) Middle East energy shock duration extending >90 days, pushing Brent above $100; (3) retaliation tariffs enacted within 30 days, creating demand shock to US agricultural and industrial exports; (4) EM currency crises via EMBI spreads >500bps and Fragile Five weakness >10 percent. Policy error risk is elevated: ECB may hike into recession, Fed may stay too long, BoE may misjudge labour market softness. Near-term (next 30 days) probability of regime cascade from Amber to Red in G10 ex-US is assessed as Medium. Tariff shock is the operative variable.

cross_monitor_candidates.signal: Middle East energy shock (Hormuz/Red Sea shipping route disruptions, oil supply vulnerability) is primary inflation driver for global macro stress regime deterioration this week. Energy price shock durability is cross-monitor material.

Trump tariff escalation (Liberation Day April 2 package) is politically driven trade policy shock. Tariff Shock Cascade Protocol activation is outcome of US domestic political economy, not economic fundamentals. Cross-monitor flagging: trade policy as political weapon.

US tariff shock directly targets EU (Section 232 metals, digital services, agriculture). ECB policy uncertainty and transatlantic decoupling protocol (per addendum Section 3) indicate structural shift in US-EU economic relationship. Financial contagion from Treasury basis trade dislocation affects ECB collateral availability.

Tariff shock applies directly to semiconductor supply chains and AI hardware imports. Data centre cost inflation from energy shock erodes AI capex profitability. No material countervailing upside from AI capex investment in 2026 macro backdrop.
cross_monitor_candidates.type: Economic coercion via export controls and tariffs

cross_monitor_candidates.signal: Middle East energy shock elevates oil prices and energy supply uncertainty. Climate transition capex deferral probable if energy costs remain elevated >6 months. Tariff shock reduces renewable energy equipment imports (China solar/wind dependency).
cross_monitor_candidates.type: Energy supply disruption + transition capex displacement
